In [ ]:
%pip install -q matplotlib seaborn pandas statistics

Part 1: Data Loading, Transformation and Exploration¶

1.1 Data Loading¶

1.1.1 SparkSession Creation¶

Write the code to create a SparkSession. For creating the SparkSession, you need to use a SparkConf object to configure the Spark app with a proper application name, to ensure the maximum partition size does not exceed 32MB, and to run locally with all CPU cores on your machine

In [1]:
# Import Library
from pyspark import SparkConf
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

# local[*]: run Spark in local mode with working processors as logical cores on your machine
master = "local[4]"

# The `appName` field is a name to be shown on the Spark cluster UI page
app_name = "Assignment-2A"

# Setup configuration parameters for Spark
spark_conf = (
    SparkConf()
    .setMaster(master)
    .setAppName(app_name)
    .set("spark.sql.files.maxPartitionBytes", 33554432) # Calculation = 32 MB x 1024 KB/MB x 1024 byte/KB = 33.554.432 bytes
)

# Define SparkSession and SparkContext
spark = SparkSession.builder.config(conf=spark_conf).getOrCreate()
sc = spark.sparkContext
sc.setLogLevel('ERROR')

# Session Timezone
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.session.timeZone", "Australia/Melbourne")

The decision to use only 4 cores (a fixed number instead of local[*]) was made because, although local[*] utilizes all available logical cores and enables faster parallelism, it is also highly memory-demanding—especially when processing large datasets such as the 13+ million records in this case.

Using fewer cores results in slower processing time but reduces memory pressure, making the process more stable and safer for handling big data.

1.1.2 Schema Definition¶

Write code to define the schemas for the datasets, following the data types suggested in the metadata file.

In [2]:
from pyspark.sql.types import (
    StructType, StructField, FloatType, 
    StringType, IntegerType, TimestampType,
    DoubleType
)

# 1. Meters
meters_schema = StructType([
    StructField("building_id",        IntegerType(),  False),
    StructField("meter_type",         StringType(),   True),
    StructField("ts",                 TimestampType(),True),
    StructField("value",              FloatType(),    True),
    StructField("row_id",             IntegerType(),  False),
])

# 2. Building Information
building_information_schema = StructType([
    StructField("site_id",            IntegerType(),  False),
    StructField("building_id",        IntegerType(),  False),
    StructField("primary_use",        StringType(),   True),
    StructField("square_feet",        IntegerType(),  True),
    StructField("floor_count",        IntegerType(),  True),
    StructField("row_id",             IntegerType(),  False),
    StructField("year_built",         IntegerType(),  True),
    StructField("latent_y",           FloatType(),    True),
    StructField("latent_s",           FloatType(),    True),
    StructField("latent_r",           FloatType(),    True),
])

# 3. Weather
weather_schema = StructType([
    StructField("site_id",            IntegerType(),   False),
    StructField("timestamp",          TimestampType(), True),
    StructField("air_temperature",    FloatType(),     True),
    StructField("cloud_coverage",     FloatType(),     True), # Result NaN if assign as IntegerType()
    StructField("dew_temperature",    FloatType(),     True),
    StructField("sea_level_pressure", FloatType(),     True), # Result NaN if assign as IntegerType()
    StructField("wind_direction",     FloatType(),     True),
    StructField("wind_speed",         FloatType(),     True),
])

The nullable feature is only set to 'True' for the unique identifier. For all other fields, it is not explicitly specified.

cloud_coverage and wind_direction are defined as FloatType() instead of IntegerType() in accordance with the metadata in the specification documents, as using IntegerType() results in errors that convert all values to NaN.

1.1.3 Load Data and Match Defined Schema¶

Using your schemas, load the CSV files into separate data frames. Print the schemas of all data frames.

In [3]:
# Import Library
from pyspark.sql import functions as F

# 1. Meters
meters_df = (
    spark.read
         .option("header", "true")
         .schema(meters_schema)
         .option("timestampFormat", "yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss[.SSS]")
         .csv("meters.csv")
)

# 2. Building Information
building_df = (
    spark.read
         .option("header", "true")
         .schema(building_information_schema)
         .option("timestampFormat", "yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss[.SSS]")
         .csv("building_information.csv")
)

# 3. Weather
weather_df = (
    spark.read
         .option("header", "true")
         .schema(weather_schema)
         .option("timestampFormat", "yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss[.SSS]")
         .csv("weather.csv")
)

# Convert columns to Integer as per required metadata
int_weather = ["cloud_coverage", "wind_direction"]

for col in int_weather:
    weather_df = weather_df.withColumn(col, F.col(col).cast(IntegerType()))

Schema Check

In [4]:
meters_df.printSchema()

root
 |-- building_id: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- meter_type: string (nullable = true)
 |-- ts: timestamp (nullable = true)
 |-- value: float (nullable = true)
 |-- row_id: integer (nullable = true)
In [5]:
building_df.printSchema()

root
 |-- site_id: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- building_id: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- primary_use: string (nullable = true)
 |-- square_feet: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- floor_count: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- row_id: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- year_built: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- latent_y: float (nullable = true)
 |-- latent_s: float (nullable = true)
 |-- latent_r: float (nullable = true)
In [6]:
weather_df.printSchema()

root
 |-- site_id: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- timestamp: timestamp (nullable = true)
 |-- air_temperature: float (nullable = true)
 |-- cloud_coverage: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- dew_temperature: float (nullable = true)
 |-- sea_level_pressure: float (nullable = true)
 |-- wind_direction: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- wind_speed: float (nullable = true)

This ensures that the schema of all loaded datasets follows the metadata description in the specification document without any errors or discrepancies.

1.2 Data Transformation to Create Features¶

In this section, we primarily have three tasks:

  • Aggregate the energy consumption to 6-hours interval
  • Impute missing values in weather dataset
  • Categorizing Peak/Off-peak months based on average air temperature

1.2.1. 6-hours Energy Consumption¶

The dataset includes sensors with hourly energy measurements. However, as a grid operator, we don’t need this level of granularity and lowering it can reduce the amount of data we need to process. For each building, we will aggregate the metered energy consumption in 6-hour intervals (0:00-5:59, 6:00-11:59, 12:00-17:59, 18:00-23:59). This will be our target (label) column for this prediction. Perform the aggregation for each building.

In [4]:
# Extract Hour and Date
meters_df = meters_df \
            .withColumn("hour", F.hour("ts")) \
            .withColumn("date", F.to_date("ts"))
In [5]:
# Compute 6-hour intervals
meters_df = meters_df.withColumn(
    "interval",
    F.when((F.col("hour") >= 0) & (F.col("hour") < 6),   "0:00-5:59")
     .when((F.col("hour") >= 6) & (F.col("hour") < 12),  "6:00-11:59")
     .when((F.col("hour") >= 12) & (F.col("hour") < 18), "12:00-17:59")
     .otherwise("18:00-23:59")
)
In [6]:
# Aggregation for each building
building_energy = (
    meters_df.groupBy("building_id", "date","interval")
             .agg(F.round(F.sum("value"), 2).alias("energy_6h"))
)
In [10]:
building_energy \
        .sort("building_id","date","interval", ascending=False) \
        .show(20)

+-----------+----------+-----------+---------+
|building_id|      date|   interval|energy_6h|
+-----------+----------+-----------+---------+
|       1448|2022-12-31| 6:00-11:59|     20.7|
|       1448|2022-12-31|18:00-23:59|     18.6|
|       1448|2022-12-31|12:00-17:59|    19.12|
|       1448|2022-12-31|  0:00-5:59|    21.55|
|       1448|2022-12-30| 6:00-11:59|    20.02|
|       1448|2022-12-30|18:00-23:59|    21.17|
|       1448|2022-12-30|12:00-17:59|    19.28|
|       1448|2022-12-30|  0:00-5:59|     20.3|
|       1448|2022-12-29| 6:00-11:59|    19.82|
|       1448|2022-12-29|18:00-23:59|    18.25|
|       1448|2022-12-29|12:00-17:59|    18.83|
|       1448|2022-12-29|  0:00-5:59|    19.75|
|       1448|2022-12-28| 6:00-11:59|    19.32|
|       1448|2022-12-28|18:00-23:59|     19.9|
|       1448|2022-12-28|12:00-17:59|    17.28|
|       1448|2022-12-28|  0:00-5:59|    21.57|
|       1448|2022-12-27| 6:00-11:59|    18.73|
|       1448|2022-12-27|18:00-23:59|    19.62|
|       1448|2022-12-27|12:00-17:59|     16.6|
|       1448|2022-12-27|  0:00-5:59|     21.0|
+-----------+----------+-----------+---------+
only showing top 20 rows

1.2.2. Impute Missing Values in Weather Data¶

In the weather dataset, there are some missing values (null or empty strings). It may lower the quality of our model. Imputation is a way to deal with those missing values. Imputation is the process of replacing missing values in a dataset with substituted, or "imputed," values. It's a way to handle gaps in your data so that you can still analyse it effectively without having to delete incomplete records.

Refer to the Spark MLLib imputation API and fill in the missing values in the weather dataset. You can use mean values as the strategy. https://spark.apache.org/docs/3.5.5/api/python/reference/api/pyspark.ml.feature.Imputer.html

In [11]:
# Understand the Dataset
weather_df.toPandas().describe(include='all')

/opt/conda/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pyspark/sql/pandas/types.py:563: FutureWarning: is_datetime64tz_dtype is deprecated and will be removed in a future version. Check `isinstance(dtype, pd.DatetimeTZDtype)` instead.
  if not is_datetime64tz_dtype(pser.dtype):
/opt/conda/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pyspark/sql/pandas/types.py:379: FutureWarning: is_datetime64tz_dtype is deprecated and will be removed in a future version. Check `isinstance(dtype, pd.DatetimeTZDtype)` instead.
  if is_datetime64tz_dtype(s.dtype):
Out[11]:
site_id timestamp air_temperature cloud_coverage dew_temperature sea_level_pressure wind_direction wind_speed
count 139773.000000 139773 139718.000000 70600.000000 139660.000000 129155.000000 133505.000000 139469.000000
mean 7.478977 2022-07-02 11:08:07.098366720 14.418106 2.149306 7.350158 1016.158020 180.526632 3.560527
min 0.000000 2022-01-01 00:00:00 -28.900000 0.000000 -35.000000 968.200012 0.000000 0.000000
25% 3.000000 2022-04-02 02:00:00 7.200000 0.000000 0.600000 1011.799988 80.000000 2.100000
50% 7.000000 2022-07-02 09:00:00 15.000000 2.000000 8.300000 1016.400024 190.000000 3.100000
75% 11.000000 2022-10-01 16:00:00 22.200001 4.000000 14.400000 1020.799988 280.000000 5.000000
max 15.000000 2022-12-31 23:00:00 47.200001 9.000000 26.100000 1045.500000 360.000000 19.000000
std 4.604744 NaN 10.626663 2.599150 9.790941 7.629611 111.523629 2.335962

From the describe results, we can observe that the columns in weather_df contain different types of numerical data:

  • Continuous numerical data: air_temperature, dew_temperature, sea_level_pressure, and wind_speed — these show decimal values in the minimum, quartile, and maximum statistics.
  • Discrete numerical data: cloud_coverage and wind_direction — these display only whole numbers in the minimum, quartile, and maximum statistics.

For imputation, the strategy should be assigned according to the data type:

  • Continuous numerical data → imputed using the mean.
  • Discrete numerical data → imputed using the median or mode.
In [7]:
# Import Imputer Library
from pyspark.ml.feature import Imputer
In [8]:
# Continuous columns (mean)
impute_mean = ["air_temperature", "dew_temperature", "sea_level_pressure", "wind_speed"]

imputer_mean = Imputer(
    inputCols  = impute_mean,
    outputCols = impute_mean,
    strategy   = "mean"
)

# Apply mean imputation
weather_imputed = imputer_mean.fit(weather_df).transform(weather_df)
In [9]:
# Discrete columns (median)
impute_median = ["cloud_coverage", "wind_direction"]


imputer_median = Imputer(
    inputCols  = impute_median,
    outputCols = impute_median,
    strategy   = "median"
)

# Apply mean imputation
weather_imputed = imputer_median.fit(weather_imputed).transform(weather_imputed)
In [15]:
# Count the Missing Values
exclude_cols = {"site_id", "timestamp"}

# Count Missing Values per column
missing_counts = weather_imputed.select([
    F.count(
        F.when(
            F.col(c).isNull() | F.isnan(F.col(c)) | (F.col(c) == ''), 
            1
        )
    ).alias(c)
    for c in weather_imputed.columns if c not in exclude_cols
])

missing_counts.show(truncate=True, vertical=True)

-RECORD 0-----------------
 air_temperature    | 0   
 cloud_coverage     | 0   
 dew_temperature    | 0   
 sea_level_pressure | 0   
 wind_direction     | 0   
 wind_speed         | 0

After imputation, all numerical columns in weather_df have been filled, and no missing values remain.

1.2.3. Categorization Peak Months by Average Air Temperature¶

We know that different seasons may affect energy consumption—for instance, a heater in winter and a cooler in summer. Extracting peak seasons (summer and winter) or off-peak seasons (Spring and Autumn) might be more useful than directly using the month as numerical values.

The dataset has 16 sites in total, whose locations may span across different countries. Add a column (peak/off-peak) to the weather data frame based on the average air temperature. The top 3 hottest months and the 3 coldest months are considered “peak”, and the rest of the year is considered “off-peak”.

In [10]:
# Extract Month
weather_imputed = weather_imputed \
                    .withColumn('month', F.month('timestamp')) \
                    .withColumn('year', F.year('timestamp'))
In [11]:
# Compute Monthly Average (each year) of `air_temperature`
season_peak = (
    weather_imputed.groupBy('site_id','year','month')
             .agg(F.round(F.avg('air_temperature'), 2).alias("average_temp"))
)
In [12]:
# Import Library for Window process [1]
from pyspark.sql.window import Window

# Rank each row by temperature within each year group
# Define window rules
# Hottest Month
w_hot = Window.partitionBy("site_id").orderBy(F.col("average_temp").desc())
# Coldest Month
w_cold = Window.partitionBy("site_id").orderBy(F.col("average_temp").asc())

season_peak = (season_peak
    .withColumn("hot_rank", F.dense_rank().over(w_hot))
    .withColumn("cold_rank", F.dense_rank().over(w_cold))
    .withColumn("season_type", 
                    F.when((F.col("hot_rank") <= 3) | (F.col("cold_rank") <= 3), "peak")
                     .otherwise("off-peak")
               )
)
In [19]:
# Check Rank result
season_peak.filter('year = 2022 AND site_id == 15').show()

+-------+----+-----+------------+--------+---------+-----------+
|site_id|year|month|average_temp|hot_rank|cold_rank|season_type|
+-------+----+-----+------------+--------+---------+-----------+
|     15|2022|    1|       -3.41|      12|        1|       peak|
|     15|2022|    2|       -1.85|      11|        2|       peak|
|     15|2022|   12|       -1.47|      10|        3|       peak|
|     15|2022|    3|        4.19|       9|        4|   off-peak|
|     15|2022|   11|        5.28|       8|        5|   off-peak|
|     15|2022|    4|        5.37|       7|        6|   off-peak|
|     15|2022|   10|       10.65|       6|        7|   off-peak|
|     15|2022|    5|        13.4|       5|        8|   off-peak|
|     15|2022|    9|        17.3|       4|        9|   off-peak|
|     15|2022|    6|       18.54|       3|       10|       peak|
|     15|2022|    7|       21.83|       2|       11|       peak|
|     15|2022|    8|       22.33|       1|       12|       peak|
+-------+----+-----+------------+--------+---------+-----------+

The results are as expected: the peak season is assigned to the top three hottest months (which also correspond to the three coldest months of the year in reverse), and the opposite is applied otherwise.

1.2.4. Features DataFrame Creation¶

Using all relevant columns from the previous steps, we built feature_df as the main DataFrame for EDA, analysis, and modeling.

In [13]:
# Dataset Preparation
# Season Peak
season_peak_join = season_peak.select('site_id','month','year','season_type')

# Weather
weather_join = weather_imputed

# Building Energy
building_energy_join = building_energy

# Building Info
building_join = building_df.drop("row_id")

# Meters
meters_join = meters_df.select('building_id','date','interval')

Justification:

  • For season_peak_join, we only need the categorization peak/off-peak (season_type) for each month-year (month, year) that used later as keys to join for other dataframe with also primary key of site_id

  • The row_id columns from building_df and meters_df are excluded since they are simply record indices in each table and have no analytical relevance.

  • For meters_join, only building_id and date are retained as identifiers to connect with other dataframes. The interval column is also kept as it may serve as an important feature while reducing modeling complexity.

Data Cleaning¶

This step aims to check whether the initial DataFrames to be joined contain duplicates or missing values. This prevents unnecessary duplication during joins, which could lead to a larger and misleading DataFrame.

Note: Data cleaning for duplicates and missing values is also performed after each join to ensure correctness.

Duplicated Data¶
In [14]:
# Import Library
from pyspark.sql.functions import isnan, when, count, col
In [15]:
def find_duplicates(df, show=True):
    """
    Finds duplicate rows in DataFrame.

    Parameters:
        df (DataFrame): The PySpark DataFrame to check for duplicates.
        show (bool): Whether to display the duplicate rows. Default is True.

    Returns:
        duplicates_df: A new DataFrame containing only the duplicate rows and their counts.
    """
    duplicates_df = (
        df.groupBy(df.columns)
          .count()
          .filter("count > 1")
    )
    
    if show:
        duplicates_df.show(truncate=False,vertical=True)
    
    return duplicates_df
In [23]:
find_duplicates(season_peak_join)

(0 rows)
Out[23]:
DataFrame[site_id: int, month: int, year: int, season_type: string, count: bigint]
In [24]:
find_duplicates(weather_join)

(0 rows)
Out[24]:
DataFrame[site_id: int, timestamp: timestamp, air_temperature: float, cloud_coverage: int, dew_temperature: float, sea_level_pressure: float, wind_direction: int, wind_speed: float, month: int, year: int, count: bigint]
In [25]:
find_duplicates(building_energy_join)

(0 rows)
Out[25]:
DataFrame[building_id: int, date: date, interval: string, energy_6h: double, count: bigint]
In [26]:
find_duplicates(building_join)

(0 rows)
Out[26]:
DataFrame[site_id: int, building_id: int, primary_use: string, square_feet: int, floor_count: int, year_built: int, latent_y: float, latent_s: float, latent_r: float, count: bigint]
In [27]:
find_duplicates(meters_join)

only showing top 20 rows
Out[27]:
DataFrame[building_id: int, date: date, interval: string, count: bigint]

These duplicates appear because row_id was dropped (since it is only relevant to meters_df) and because we created interval that mainly to reduce complexity and granularity for modeling, some records within the same interval ended up being labeled as duplicates.

In [16]:
# Drop Duplicate
clean_meters = meters_join.dropDuplicates()
In [29]:
find_duplicates(clean_meters)

(0 rows)
Out[29]:
DataFrame[building_id: int, date: date, interval: string, count: bigint]

Duplicated records in meters_join were dropped to prevent unnecessary data accumulation and reduce processing overhead. And other dataframe are no duplicated data present.

Missing Values¶
In [30]:
season_peak_join.toPandas().isnull().sum()
Out[30]:
site_id        0
month          0
year           0
season_type    0
dtype: int64
In [31]:
weather_join.toPandas().isnull().sum()

/opt/conda/lib/python3.10/site-packages/pyspark/sql/pandas/types.py:379: FutureWarning: is_datetime64tz_dtype is deprecated and will be removed in a future version. Check `isinstance(dtype, pd.DatetimeTZDtype)` instead.

dtype: int64

There are no missing values in any of the datasets that will be joined later.

Join Datasets¶

JOIN 1: weather_join & season_peak_join¶
In [17]:
# Join Weather with Season Peak 
weather_peak = weather_join.join(
    season_peak_join,
    ['site_id','year','month'],       # [2]
    'left'
) \
.drop('month','year')

weather_peak.printSchema()

root
 |-- site_id: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- timestamp: timestamp (nullable = true)
 |-- air_temperature: float (nullable = true)
 |-- cloud_coverage: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- dew_temperature: float (nullable = true)
 |-- sea_level_pressure: float (nullable = true)
 |-- wind_direction: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- wind_speed: float (nullable = true)
 |-- season_type: string (nullable = true)

To simplify the granularity of the data, analysis, and modelling later, interval aggregation of numerical data per 6 hour intervals are implement similar to steps for building_energy.

In [18]:
# Extract Hour and Date
weather_peak = weather_peak \
            .withColumn("hour", F.hour("timestamp")) \
            .withColumn("date", F.to_date("timestamp")) \
            .drop('timestamp')

# Compute 6-hour intervals
weather_peak = weather_peak.withColumn(
    "interval",
    F.when((F.col("hour") >= 0) & (F.col("hour") < 6),   "0:00-5:59")
     .when((F.col("hour") >= 6) & (F.col("hour") < 12),  "6:00-11:59")
     .when((F.col("hour") >= 12) & (F.col("hour") < 18), "12:00-17:59")
     .otherwise("18:00-23:59")
)

# Aggregation for each sites
weather_peak_agg = (
    weather_peak.groupBy("site_id", "date", "interval")
    .agg(
    *[
        F.round(F.mean(c), 2).alias(f"avg_{c}_6h")
        for c in impute_mean
    ],
    *[
        F.round(F.mode(c), 2).alias(f"mode_{c}_6h")
        for c in impute_median    
    ],
    F.first("season_type").alias("season_type"))
).drop('avg_site_id_6h')

As discussed in 1.2.2 Imputation, continuous data (e.g., air_temperature, dew_temperature) are best aggregated using the mean.

In contrast, discrete data (cloud_coverage, wind_direction) are more suitable for aggregation by mode, particularly since these columns have a fixed range of discrete values as specified in the assignment metadata."

In [37]:
weather_peak_agg.printSchema()

root
 |-- site_id: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- date: date (nullable = true)
 |-- interval: string (nullable = false)
 |-- avg_air_temperature_6h: double (nullable = true)
 |-- avg_dew_temperature_6h: double (nullable = true)
 |-- avg_sea_level_pressure_6h: double (nullable = true)
 |-- avg_wind_speed_6h: double (nullable = true)
 |-- mode_cloud_coverage_6h: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- mode_wind_direction_6h: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- season_type: string (nullable = true)
Data Cleaning¶
In [38]:
find_duplicates(weather_peak_agg)

(0 rows)
Out[38]:
DataFrame[site_id: int, date: date, interval: string, avg_air_temperature_6h: double, avg_dew_temperature_6h: double, avg_sea_level_pressure_6h: double, avg_wind_speed_6h: double, mode_cloud_coverage_6h: int, mode_wind_direction_6h: int, season_type: string, count: bigint]
In [39]:
weather_peak_agg.toPandas().isnull().sum()

The joined dataframe of weather_peak_agg doesn't have any duplicated and missing value.

JOIN 2: clean_meters & building_energy_join¶
In [19]:
# Add `energy_6h` to meters 
meters_energy = clean_meters.join(
    building_energy_join,
    ['building_id','date','interval'],       # [2]
    'left'
)

meters_energy.printSchema()

root
 |-- building_id: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- date: date (nullable = true)
 |-- interval: string (nullable = false)
 |-- energy_6h: double (nullable = true)
Data Cleaning¶

(0 rows)

JOIN 3: meters_energy_clean & building_join¶
In [20]:
# Join Meters_Energy with Building Info
meters_energy_info = meters_energy.join(
    building_join,
    ['building_id'],       
    'left'
)

meters_energy_info.printSchema()

root
 |-- building_id: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- date: date (nullable = true)
 |-- interval: string (nullable = false)
 |-- energy_6h: double (nullable = true)
 |-- site_id: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- primary_use: string (nullable = true)
 |-- square_feet: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- floor_count: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- year_built: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- latent_y: float (nullable = true)
 |-- latent_s: float (nullable = true)
 |-- latent_r: float (nullable = true)
Data Cleaning¶
In [44]:
find_duplicates(meters_energy_info)

(0 rows)
Out[44]:
DataFrame[building_id: int, date: date, interval: string, energy_6h: double, site_id: int, primary_use: string, square_feet: int, floor_count: int, year_built: int, latent_y: float, latent_s: float, latent_r: float, count: bigint]
In [45]:
meters_energy_info.toPandas().isnull().sum()
Out[45]:
building_id    0
date           0
interval       0
energy_6h      0
site_id        0
primary_use    0
square_feet    0
floor_count    0
year_built     0
latent_y       0
latent_s       0

dtype: int64

The joined dataframe of meters_energy_info doesn't have any duplicated and missing value.

JOIN 4: weather_peak_agg and meters_energy_info¶
In [21]:
# Rename one of the `site_id` to avoid duplication
weather_peak_agg = weather_peak_agg \
                    .withColumnRenamed("site_id", "weather_site_id") \
                    .withColumnRenamed("interval", "weather_interval") \
                    .withColumnRenamed("date", "weather_date")

# Create feature_df
feature_df = meters_energy_info.join(
    weather_peak_agg,
    (meters_energy_info.site_id == weather_peak_agg.weather_site_id) &
    (meters_energy_info.date == weather_peak_agg.weather_date) &
    (meters_energy_info.interval == weather_peak_agg.weather_interval),
    "left"
).drop("weather_site_id","weather_interval","weather_date")


# Drop Duplicate
feature_df = feature_df.dropDuplicates()
feature_df = feature_df.dropna()

feature_df.printSchema()

root
 |-- building_id: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- date: date (nullable = true)
 |-- interval: string (nullable = false)
 |-- energy_6h: double (nullable = true)
 |-- site_id: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- primary_use: string (nullable = true)
 |-- square_feet: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- floor_count: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- year_built: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- latent_y: float (nullable = true)
 |-- latent_s: float (nullable = true)
 |-- latent_r: float (nullable = true)
 |-- avg_air_temperature_6h: double (nullable = true)
 |-- avg_dew_temperature_6h: double (nullable = true)
 |-- avg_sea_level_pressure_6h: double (nullable = true)
 |-- avg_wind_speed_6h: double (nullable = true)
 |-- mode_cloud_coverage_6h: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- mode_wind_direction_6h: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- season_type: string (nullable = true)

The join between meters_energy_info and weather_peak_agg produces two site_id columns. To simplify, one must be removed, and it is safer to drop the second one. To avoid duplication, it is preferable to rename site_id in weather_peak.

Subsequently, timestamp and weather_site_id from weather_peak also be dropped, since the corresponding columns from meters_energy_info are retained as it serves as the main dataset.

Data Cleaning¶
In [47]:
find_duplicates(feature_df)

(0 rows)

1.3 Exploring the Data ¶

You can use either the CDA or the EDA method mentioned in Lab 5.

Some ideas for CDA:
a) Older building may not be as efficient as new ones, therefore need more energy for cooling/heating. It’s not necessarily true though, if the buildings are built with higher standard or renovated later.
b) A multifloored or larger building obviously consumes more energy.

In [22]:
# Import Plot Libraries
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import statistics
import pandas as pd
import seaborn as sns

# Inline display of graphs in Jupyter Notebook
%matplotlib notebook
In [50]:
# Convert to Pandas for Plot Visualization
pandas_df = feature_df.toPandas()

b) For each non-numeric column, display the top-5 values and the corresponding counts

In [54]:
def categorical_summary(df, top_n):
    """
    Displays the top-N most frequent values for each non-numeric column in a Spark DataFrame.
    """
    
    numeric_types = {"int", "bigint", "integer", "smallint", "double", "float", "decimal", "long", "boolean"}
    non_numeric_cols = [c for c, t in df.dtypes if t.lower() not in numeric_types]
    
    for c in non_numeric_cols:
        print("="*50)
        print(f"Top {top_n} values for column: {c}")

        df.groupBy(c) \
          .count() \
          .orderBy(col("count").desc()) \
          .show(top_n, truncate=False)
In [55]:
categorical_summary(feature_df, 5)

==================================================

|off-peak   |714542|
|peak       |707964|
+-----------+------+

c) For each boolean column, display the value and count. (note: pandas describe is allowed for this task.)

In [56]:
def boolean_summary(df):
    """
    For each boolean column in a Spark DataFrame, 
    display counts of True/False values using Pandas.
    """
    
    # Identify boolean columns
    bool_cols = [c for c, t in df.dtypes if t == "boolean"]

    if not bool_cols:
        print("No boolean columns found.")
        return

    # Convert only boolean cols to Pandas
    pdf = df.select(bool_cols).toPandas()

    for c in bool_cols:
        print("="*50)
        print(f"Boolean counts for column: {c}")
        print(pdf[c].value_counts(dropna=False))
In [57]:
boolean_summary(feature_df)

No boolean columns found.

Since there are no boolean columns in the initial datasets or in feature_df, a boolean summary resulting as none.

However, some features can logically be represented as boolean. For example, season_type can be encoded as 1 for peak and 0 for off-peak. To achieve this, the column can be transformed using one-hot encoding, which will be applied in the following steps.

1.3.2 Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)¶

Explore the dataframe and write code to present two plots of multivariate analysis, describe your plots and discuss the findings from the plots. (5% each).

  • 150 words max for each plot’s description and discussion.
  • Note: In the building metadata table, there are some latent columns (data that may or may not be helpful, their meanings is unknown due to privacy and data security concerns).
  • Feel free to use any plotting libraries: matplotlib, seabon, plotly, etc. You can refer to https://samplecode.link

Multivariate Plot #1 : Corellation between Features to Target (energy_6h)¶

In [58]:
# Numerical columns
numerical_cols = [
    "energy_6h",
    "square_feet",
    "floor_count",
    "year_built",
    "latent_y",
    "latent_s",
    "latent_r",
    "avg_air_temperature_6h",
    "mode_cloud_coverage_6h",
    "avg_dew_temperature_6h",
    "avg_sea_level_pressure_6h",
    "mode_wind_direction_6h",
    "avg_wind_speed_6h",
]
In [59]:
# compute + sort
corr_with_target = pandas_df[numerical_cols].corr()['energy_6h'].drop('energy_6h')
corr_sorted = corr_with_target.reindex(corr_with_target.abs().sort_values(ascending=False).index)

# plot
plt.figure(figsize=(9,6))
ax = sns.barplot(
    x=corr_sorted.values,
    y=corr_sorted.index,
    hue=corr_sorted.values,
    palette="coolwarm",
    dodge=False,
    legend=False
)
ax.axvline(0, color="black", linewidth=0.8)
ax.set_title("Correlation of Features with energy_6h")
ax.set_xlabel("Correlation coefficient")
ax.set_ylabel("Features")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

Discussion
A bar plot is used as it is widely applied and effective for showing the impact of each numerical feature on the target variable, energy_6h. The correlation results reveal several points:

  • All correlations are small, which is expected given the large dataset and the variability across different site_id and building_id (overall correlation max at 0.04).
  • square_feet shows the strongest positive correlation, which is reasonable since larger buildings consume more energy.
  • latent_ features, though undefined in the metadata, appear in the top five correlations and are retained for training.
  • Weather features show relatively low correlations, even though temperature logically affects energy use. The scale and variability of the dataset explain the weak results.
  • floor_count is also in the top ten, aligning with expectations that more floors increase energy demand through additional equipment and systems.

Multivariate Analysis #2 : Relationship between square_feet and energy_6h based on site_id and building_id¶

In [60]:
# Plot for All Data
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
scatter = plt.scatter(
    pandas_df["square_feet"],
    pandas_df["energy_6h"],
    c=pandas_df["site_id"],
    cmap="tab20",
    alpha=0.7,
    edgecolors='w'
)

plt.xlabel("Square Feet")
plt.ylabel("Energy Usage (6h)")
plt.title("Energy Usage vs Square Footage (Colored by Site ID)")
plt.colorbar(scatter, label="Site ID")
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()

In [81]:
# Filter out building_id = 1099
filtered_df = pandas_df[pandas_df["building_id"] != 1099]

# Plot for All Data (excluding building 1099)
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
scatter = plt.scatter(
    filtered_df["square_feet"],
    filtered_df["energy_6h"],
    c=filtered_df["site_id"],
    cmap="tab20",
    alpha=0.7,
    edgecolors='w'
)

plt.xlabel("Square Feet")
plt.ylabel("Energy Usage (6h)")
plt.title("Energy Usage vs Square Footage (Excluding Building ID 1099)")
plt.colorbar(scatter, label="Site ID")
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()
In [82]:
# Filter out site_id = 13
filtered_df = pandas_df[pandas_df["site_id"] != 13]

# Plot for All Data (excluding building 1099)
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
scatter = plt.scatter(
    filtered_df["square_feet"],
    filtered_df["energy_6h"],
    c=filtered_df["site_id"],
    cmap="tab20",
    alpha=0.7,
    edgecolors='w'
)

plt.xlabel("Square Feet")
plt.ylabel("Energy Usage (6h)")
plt.title("Energy Usage vs Square Footage (Excluding Site ID 13)")
plt.colorbar(scatter, label="Site ID")
plt.grid(True)
plt.show()

Discussion
To explore the influence of square_feet on energy_6h, we divided the data by site_id and building_id to check whether patterns were generalizable or unique. Scatter plots were used to reveal clustering and multivariate relationships.

Initial results showed discrepancies, with site_id = 13 displaying extremely diverse energy consumption while others appeared flat. A statistical summary confirmed that site_id = 13 had much higher mean and max values. Box plots were then used to examine building_id within site_id = 13, revealing that building_id = 1099 was the main outlier. Excluding it provided clearer patterns, though many sites remained flat. Excluding site_id = 13 entirely gave a much better representation: for example, site_id = 3 showed low energy_6h across sizes, site_id = 14 was consistently high, and site_id = 7 moderate-high.

These findings indicate that site_id strongly influences energy_6h, reflecting local conditions and equipment specifications.

Part 2. Feature extraction and ML training ¶

In this section, you must use PySpark DataFrame functions and ML packages for data preparation, model building, and evaluation. Other ML packages, such as scikit-learn, should not be used to process the data; however, it’s fine to use them to display the result or evaluate your model.

2.1 Discuss the feature selection and prepare the feature columns¶

2.1.1. Feature Selections¶

Based on the data exploration from 1.2 and considering the use case, discuss the importance of those features (For example, which features may be useless and should be removed, which feature has a significant impact on the label column, which should be transformed), which features you are planning to use?

Discuss the reasons for selecting them and how you plan to create/transform them.

  • 300 words max for the discussion
  • Please only use the provided data for model building
  • You can create/add additional features based on the dataset

Hint - Use the insights from the data exploration/domain knowledge/statistical models to consider whether to create more feature columns, whether to remove some columns

In [83]:
feature_df.printSchema()

root
 |-- building_id: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- date: date (nullable = true)
 |-- interval: string (nullable = false)
 |-- energy_6h: double (nullable = true)
 |-- site_id: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- primary_use: string (nullable = true)
 |-- square_feet: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- floor_count: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- year_built: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- latent_y: float (nullable = true)
 |-- latent_s: float (nullable = true)
 |-- latent_r: float (nullable = true)
 |-- avg_air_temperature_6h: double (nullable = true)
 |-- avg_dew_temperature_6h: double (nullable = true)
 |-- avg_sea_level_pressure_6h: double (nullable = true)
 |-- avg_wind_speed_6h: double (nullable = true)
 |-- mode_cloud_coverage_6h: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- mode_wind_direction_6h: integer (nullable = true)
 |-- season_type: string (nullable = true)

Feature Inclusion
Based on the join justification and data exploration, the selected features are:

  • energy_6h: target variable.

  • site_id and building_id: Categorical identifiers capturing location-specific usage, assuming the model is applied only for within-sample forecasting.

  • Latent features (latent_y, latent_s, latent_r): Definitions unknown but strongly correlated with energy_6h, suggesting sensor or operational signals.

  • interval: Key identifier for contextual categorization of energy use.

  • Aggregated weather features (avg_air_temperature_6h, avg_wind_speed_6h, etc.): Retained due to logical HVAC influence and observed correlations.

  • primary_use and season_type: Categoricaldescriptors of building function and temporal seasonality.

  • square_feet and floor_count: Physical building attributes with strong correlation to energy demand.

  • year_built: May indicate efficiency, as older buildings often consume more due to outdated equipment.

Features Exclusion

  • row_id (from building_df and meters_df): Surrogate index with no analytical or predictive value.

  • date: Excluded as redundant; interval better represents the 6-hour aggregation.

Feature Transformation
This part mainly influenced by the result of statistical summary:

  • season_type: Binary encoded using OneHotEncoder (peak/off-peak).

  • interval and primary_use: Transformed with StringIndexer for multi-class categories.

  • building_id and site_id: Retained as numeric identifiers; tree models handle unordered categories.

  • Scaling/Normalization: Not applied. Tree-based models (RF, GBT) are scale-invariant and preserve raw feature patterns [3].

  • Imputation: Not required, as feature_df contains no missing values.

Other Considerations

  • Outlier: Though site_id = 13 shows anomalies in multivariate analysis, it is retained to preserve variation and allow the model to learn unique behavior.

Final Note
All features (except date) are kept as a baseline; feature importance after training will guide further refinement.

2.1.2 Create Features Categorization¶

Write code to create/transform the columns based on your discussion above at Section 2.1.1.

In [23]:
# Target column (label)
label_col = "energy_6h"

# Categorical features
# String Indexer
categorical_SI = ["primary_use", "interval","site_id","building_id"]
# One-Hot Encoder
categorical_OHE = ["season_type"]

# Numerical features
numeric_cols = [
    # building structure
    "square_feet", "floor_count", "year_built",           
    # unknown but relevant sensor signals
    "latent_y", "latent_s", "latent_r",                   
    # weather
    "avg_air_temperature_6h", "avg_dew_temperature_6h",   
    "avg_sea_level_pressure_6h", "avg_wind_speed_6h",
    "mode_cloud_coverage_6h", "mode_wind_direction_6h"
]

2.2 Preparing Spark ML Transformers/Estimators for features, labels, and models¶

2.2.1 Transformers and Estimators¶

Write code to create Transformers/Estimators for transforming/assembling the columns you selected above in 2.1 and create ML model Estimators for Random Forest (RF) and Gradient-boosted tree (GBT) model.

Please DO NOT fit/transform the data yet.

In [24]:
# Import Library
from pyspark.ml.feature import StringIndexer, OneHotEncoder, VectorAssembler
from pyspark.ml.linalg import Vectors
from pyspark.ml.regression import RandomForestRegressor, GBTRegressor
In [25]:
# String Indexer
indexers = [
    StringIndexer(inputCol=col, outputCol=f"{col}_SI").setHandleInvalid("keep")
    # All categorical (non-integer) need to be indexed
    for col in categorical_SI + categorical_OHE 
]
In [26]:
# One-Hot Encoder
encoders = [
    OneHotEncoder(
        inputCol=f"{col}_SI",
        outputCol=f"{col}_OHE"
    )
    for col in categorical_OHE
]
In [27]:
# Vector Assembler
indexed_cols = [f"{col}_SI" for col in categorical_SI]
ohe_feature_cols = [f"{col}_OHE" for col in categorical_OHE]

# Combine all feature inputs
inputCols = numeric_cols + indexed_cols + ohe_feature_cols

# Assembler
assembler = VectorAssembler(inputCols=inputCols, outputCol="features")
In [28]:
# Estimator
# Random Forest
rf = RandomForestRegressor(
    featuresCol="features",
    labelCol=label_col,
    predictionCol="rf_prediction",
    maxBins=2048 
).setSeed(2025)

# Gradient-Boosted Tree
gbt = GBTRegressor(
    featuresCol="features", 
    labelCol=label_col, 
    predictionCol="gbt_prediction",
    maxBins=2048 
).setSeed(2025)

Default parameters are used for the initial baseline model, with maxBins manually set to 2048 to accomodate the high-cardinality from site_id and building_id.

This setting ensures can accurately split on these categorical variables without error, unique patterns are captured, and avoids misleading generalization, as shown in multivariate analysis where site_id = 13, particularly building_id = 1099, behaved anomalously and flattened other patterns.

2.2.2. Pipelines¶

Write code to include the above Transformers/Estimators into two pipelines.

Please DO NOT fit/transform the data yet.

In [29]:
# Import Pipeline Library
from pyspark.ml import Pipeline
In [30]:
# Combine all transformation stages
stages = indexers + encoders + [assembler]

# Pipeline for Random Forest
pipeline_rf = Pipeline(stages=stages + [rf])

# Pipeline for Gradient-Boosted Tree
pipeline_gbt = Pipeline(stages=stages + [gbt])

2.3 Preparing the training data and testing data¶

Write code to split the data for training and testing, using 2025 as the random seed. You can decide the train/test split ratio based on the resources available on your laptop.
Note: Due to the large dataset size, you can use random sampling (say 20% of the dataset).

In [31]:
# Splitting the data into testing and training set 80% into training and 20% for testing
train, test = feature_df.randomSplit([0.8, 0.2], seed=2025)

2.4 Training and evaluating models¶

2.4.1 Write code to use the corresponding ML Pipelines to train the models on the training data from 2.3. And then use the trained models to predict the testing data from 2.3

2.4.1. Train Model¶

In [32]:
def display_feature_importance(pipeline_model, assembler):
    """
    Display sorted feature importances from a trained PipelineModel with tree-based regressor.
    
    Parameters:
    - pipeline_model: Trained PipelineModel (e.g., rf_model or gbt_model)
    - assembler: VectorAssembler used in the pipeline (must match the one used during training)
    """
    # Extract last model stage (assumed to be a regressor)
    regressor_model = pipeline_model.stages[-1]
    
    # Get feature importances from the tree-based regressor
    importances = regressor_model.featureImportances
    
    # Original feature names used in VectorAssembler
    inputCols = assembler.getInputCols()
    n_input_cols = len(inputCols)
    n_features = len(importances)

    # Expand feature names (assume 2 OHE dimensions per categorical feature)
    expanded_feature_names = []
    for col in inputCols:
        if col.endswith("_OHE"):
            expanded_feature_names.extend([f"{col}_0", f"{col}_1"])
        else:
            expanded_feature_names.append(col)

    # Truncate if expansion exceeds the number of features
    expanded_feature_names = expanded_feature_names[:n_features]

    # Create dictionary and sort by importance
    feature_importance_dict = {
        expanded_feature_names[i]: round(float(imp), 4)
        for i, imp in enumerate(importances)
    }

    sorted_features = sorted(feature_importance_dict.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)
    
    # Get The Model Name
    model_name = regressor_model.__class__.__name__
    
    # Display results
    print(f"{model_name} Feature Importances:")
    for feat, score in sorted_features:
        print(f"{feat}: {score}")

Random Forest¶

In [ ]:
# Random Forest
rf_model = pipeline_rf.fit(train)
In [ ]:
# Feature Importance
display_feature_importance(rf_model, assembler)

Findings:

  • Building Features:
    square_feet (0.2309) has strong influences, while floor_count (0.0295) and year_built (0.0287) are moderately influential. Confirming that physical building characteristics affect energy use. Consistent with the earlier correlation matrix.

  • Latent Variables:
    latent_s (0.066) shows moderate importance, while latent_y (0.0091) and latent_r (0.0) contribute weak to none. Despite its unknown definition, possibly capturing sensor or behavioral patterns related to enegy_6h

  • Categorical Features:
    season_type_OHE_0 (0.0899), season_type_OHE_1 (0.0544), and primary_use_SI (0.0342) are meaningful predictors, capturing temporal and functional usage patterns.

  • Weather features:
    Influence is generally modest:

    • avg_sea_level_pressure_6h (0.054), avg_air_temperature_6h (0.0353),avg_dew_temperature_6h (0.0237), avg_wind_speed_6h (0.0246)
    • mode_wind_direction_6h (0.014), mode_cloud_coverage_6h (0.0048) are nearly negligible.

  • Site & Interval Features:
    building_id_SI (0.2706) dominates the model, suggesting site-specific patterns are crucial. site_id_SI (0.028) showk weak influences, and interval_SI (0.0024) has negligible contribution.

Next, a comparison between the full model and a reduced model (using features with importance ≥ 0.03) should be conducted against RMSLE before hyperparameter tuning to assess effectiveness.

Gradient-Boosted Tree¶

In [ ]:
# Gradient-Boosted Tree
gbt_model = pipeline_gbt.fit(train)
In [ ]:
# Feature Importance
display_feature_importance(gbt_model, assembler)

Findings:

  • Weather Features:
    GBT captures nonlinear patterns effectively, making weather variables the most influential:

    • avg_dew_temperature_6h (0.1955)
    • avg_sea_level_pressure_6h (0.1916)
    • avg_air_temperature_6h (0.174)
    • mode_wind_direction_6h (0.1058)
    • avg_wind_speed_6h (0.0761) show moderate importance
    • mode_cloud_coverage_6h (0.0169) is weak

  • Building & Site Features:
    building_id_SI (0.1651) remains a strong predictor, likely capturing site-specific energy behaviors.

In contrast, square_feet, floor_count, year_built, and site_id_SI all show zero influence, suggesting physical structure contributes less when rich weather data is present.

  • Latent Features:
    All latent features (latent_y, latent_s, latent_r) contribute nothing to model performance, implying GBT may not benefit from hidden representation in this setup.

  • Categorical features:
    season_type_OHE_0 (0.0545) contributes modestly, while season_type_OHE_1 and primary_use_SI show zero importance, possibly due to their redundancy with dominant weather patterns.

  • Interval Feature:
    interval_SI (0.0206) shows weak influence, consistent with previous models.

Next, we will compare the full model with a reduced model (features ≥ 0.03 importance) using RMSLE before hyperparameter tuning to evaluate effectiveness.

Prediction¶

In [ ]:
# Random Forest
rf_predictions = rf_model.transform(test)
In [ ]:
rf_predictions.select('features', 'energy_6h', 'rf_prediction').show(5)
In [ ]:
# Gradient-Boosted Tree
gbt_predictions = gbt_model.transform(test)
In [ ]:
gbt_predictions.select('features', 'energy_6h', 'gbt_prediction').show(5)

2.4.2 Model Evaluation¶

For both models (RF and GBT): with the test data, decide on which metrics to use for model evaluation and discuss which one is the better model (no word limit; please keep it concise). You may also use a plot for visualisation (not mandatory).

$$ \epsilon = \sqrt{ \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left( \log(p_i + 1) - \log(a_i + 1) \right)^2 } $$

where:

  • $\epsilon$: 𝑅𝑀𝑆𝐿𝐸 𝑠𝑐𝑜𝑟e
  • $n$:T𝑜𝑡𝑎𝑙 𝑛𝑢𝑚𝑏𝑒𝑟 𝑜𝑓 𝑑𝑎𝑡𝑎𝑝oints 𝑖𝑛 𝑡ℎ𝑒 𝑑𝑎𝑡𝑎𝑠𝑒t
  • $p_i$: P𝑟𝑒𝑑𝑖𝑐𝑡𝑖𝑜𝑛 𝑡𝑎𝑟𝑔𝑒t
  • $a_i$: Actual target
  • $\log(x)$: Natural Logarithm of x
In [33]:
def rmsle(pred_df, labelCol="energy_6h", predictionCol="prediction", verbose=False):
    df = pred_df.withColumn(
        "squared_log_error",
        (F.log1p(F.col(predictionCol)) - F.log1p(F.col(labelCol)))**2
    )
    rmsle_val = (
        df.agg(F.sqrt(F.mean("squared_log_error")).alias("rmsle"))
          .collect()[0]["rmsle"]
    )
    if verbose:
        df.select(labelCol, predictionCol, "squared_log_error").show(5)
    return rmsle_val
In [ ]:
rf_rmsle = rmsle(rf_predictions, "energy_6h", "rf_prediction")
gbt_rmsle = rmsle(gbt_predictions, "energy_6h", "gbt_prediction")

print(f"Random Forest RMSLE: {rf_rmsle:.4f}")
print(f"GBT RMSLE:           {gbt_rmsle:.4f}")

2.4.3 Best Model¶

Save the better model (you’ll need it for A2B).

(Note: You may need to go through a few training loops or use more data to create a better-performing model.)

In [ ]:
# Compare and choose the better model
if gbt_rmsle <= rf_rmsle:
    base_model = gbt
    best_model = gbt_model
    model_name = "GBT"
else:
    base_model = rf
    best_model = rf_model
    model_name = "RandomForest"
In [ ]:
best_model.write().overwrite().save(f"models/{model_name}_pipeline_model")
print(f"Best model is: {model_name}")

Discussion
The comparison shows that Gradient Boosted Trees (GBT) outperform Random Forest (RF) in predicting energy_6h.

RMSLE is used as the evaluation metric since it reduces the effect of outliers and penalizes underestimation more than overestimation, which is important here as underpredicting energy use is less acceptable than slight overprediction [4].

RF builds trees independently through bagging, often leading to bias toward dominant splits. In contrast, GBT builds trees sequentially, correcting prior errors and better capturing complex, non-linear relationships. This makes GBT more robust to imbalance and noise, though at higher computational cost.

Feature importance also highlights this difference: in RF, two features (led by building_id_SI) dominate with over 50% influence, while GBT distributes influence more evenly across the top four features. This indicates that RF is biased toward strong categorical splits, whereas GBT provides more balanced learning and stronger generalization.

Part 3. Hyperparameter Tuning and Model Optimisation ¶

Apply the techniques you have learnt from the labs, for example, CrossValidator, TrainValidationSplit, ParamGridBuilder, etc., to perform further hyperparameter tuning and model optimisation.

The assessment is based on the quality of your work/process, not the quality of your model. Please include your thoughts/ideas/discussions.

In [ ]:
# Import Library
from pyspark.ml.tuning import TrainValidationSplit, ParamGridBuilder
from pyspark.ml.evaluation import RegressionEvaluator
from pyspark import StorageLevel

3.1. Feature Optimization¶

Based on the feature importances from the initial full-feature model, not all features contribute meaningfully to the output. Therefore, we filter and retain only those with importance ≥ 0.03, and evaluate their performance using RMSLE to assess any differences.

In [ ]:
# Extract the trained model from the last stage of the pipeline
model_stage = best_model.stages[-1]

# Get feature importances from the actual model
importances = model_stage.featureImportances

# Get original feature names from the assembler
feature_names = assembler.getInputCols()

# Select features with importance threshold
importance_threshold = 0.03

important_features = [
    name for name, score in zip(feature_names, importances)
    if score >= importance_threshold
]
In [108]:
# Strip Tranformed Label
important_features = [name.replace('_SI', '').replace('_OHE', '') for name in important_features]
important_features
Out[108]:
['avg_air_temperature_6h',
 'avg_dew_temperature_6h',
 'avg_sea_level_pressure_6h',
 'avg_wind_speed_6h',
 'mode_wind_direction_6h',
 'building_id',
 'season_type']
In [109]:
# Selected Features
feature_selected = feature_df.select(important_features + ["energy_6h"])

# Create Train and Test Set [0.8,0.2]
train_selected, test_selected = feature_selected.randomSplit([0.8, 0.2], seed=2025)
In [110]:
# Flexible Pipeline for Different Features
def build_pipeline(train_df, rf, gbt):
    available_cols = train_df.columns

    # Filter available features
    si_cols = [col for col in categorical_SI if col in available_cols]
    ohe_cols = [col for col in categorical_OHE if col in available_cols]
    num_cols = [col for col in numeric_cols if col in available_cols]

    # Indexers
    indexers = [
        StringIndexer(inputCol=col, outputCol=f"{col}_SI").setHandleInvalid("keep")
        for col in si_cols + ohe_cols
    ]

    # Encoders
    encoders = [
        OneHotEncoder(inputCol=f"{col}_SI", outputCol=f"{col}_OHE")
        for col in ohe_cols
    ]

    # Input features for assembler
    index_col_names = [f"{col}_SI" for col in si_cols]
    ohe_col_names = [f"{col}_OHE" for col in ohe_cols]
    inputCols = num_cols + index_col_names + ohe_col_names

    assembler = VectorAssembler(inputCols=inputCols, outputCol="features")

    # Pipelines
    stages = indexers + encoders + [assembler]
    return Pipeline(stages=stages + [rf]), Pipeline(stages=stages + [gbt])
In [111]:
pipeline_rf_selected, pipeline_gbt_selected = build_pipeline(feature_selected, rf, gbt)
In [112]:
# Train Best Model with Selected Features
if model_name == "rf":
    selected_model = pipeline_rf_selected.fit(train_selected)
else:
    selected_model = pipeline_gbt_selected.fit(train_selected)
In [113]:
# Prediction of Selected Model
selected_predictions = selected_model.transform(test_selected)
In [114]:
# Evaluation
# Determine the prediction column name based on model
prediction_col = f"{model_name.lower()}_prediction"

# Evaluate RMSLE
selected_rmsle = rmsle(selected_predictions, "energy_6h", prediction_col)
In [115]:
print(f"{model_name} (Features Impact >= {importance_threshold}) RMSLE : {selected_rmsle:.4f}")
print(f"Random Forest RMSLE                 : {rf_rmsle:.4f}")
print(f"GBT RMSLE                           : {gbt_rmsle:.4f}")

GBT (Features Impact >= 0.03) RMSLE : 1.6161
Random Forest RMSLE                 : 2.2082
GBT RMSLE                           : 1.6653

Using features selected from feature importance improves the model, reducing RMSLE to 1.6161 compared to 1.6653 with the initial full-feature model.

However, results remain unstable across runs due to:
(i) the default number of iterations,
(ii) the absence of cross-validation,
(iii) the non-deterministic nature of RandomSplit even with a fixed seed. [6]

These issues will be addressed in the next steps through hyperparameter tuning and cross-validation

3.2. Hyperparameter Tuning¶

In [127]:
# Model Parameters
print(f"{model_name} Parameters:")
selected_model_params = selected_model.stages[-1].extractParamMap()
selected_model_params = {p.name: v for p, v in selected_model_params.items()}
pd.DataFrame(selected_model_params.items(), columns=["Parameter", "Value"])

GBT Parameters:
Out[127]:
Parameter Value
0 cacheNodeIds False
1 checkpointInterval 10
2 featureSubsetStrategy all
3 featuresCol features
4 impurity variance
5 labelCol energy_6h
6 leafCol
7 lossType squared
8 maxBins 2048
9 maxDepth 5
10 maxIter 20
11 maxMemoryInMB 256
12 minInfoGain 0.0
13 minInstancesPerNode 1
14 minWeightFractionPerNode 0.0
15 predictionCol gbt_prediction
16 seed -1219566574311815711
17 stepSize 0.1
18 subsamplingRate 1.0
19 validationTol 0.01

The baseline Gradient Boosted Tree (GBT) model was trained using default parameters:

  • maxDepth = 5: Limiting the complexity of individual tress,
  • maxIter = 20: Moderate number of iterations,
  • stepSize = 0.1: Conservative learning rate,
  • subsamplingRate = 1.0: Full sample usage per iteration,
  • featureSubsetStrategy = "all": Using all features per split

Despite these defaults, the model achieved a RMSLE of 1.6161 using the selected important features, outperforming the both baseline models: Random Forest (RMSLE: 2.2082) and GBT (RMSLE: 1.6653).

However, the current configuration is likely under-optimized. The default values do not account for important dataset characteristics such as:

  • Feature skewness and imbalance (e.g., categorical dominance like building_id),
  • Potential overfitting from using all features with limited tree depth,
  • Lack of regularization or early stopping, which are often necessary for generalizability.

These limitations suggest that hyperparameter tuning (e.g., increasing maxIter, adjusting maxDepth, optimizing stepSize, or enabling subsampling) could lead to significant performance gains. The next steps will therefore include systematic tuning using CrossValidator and ParamGridBuilder to better fit the model to the data.

In [117]:
# Evaluator
evaluator = RegressionEvaluator(
    labelCol="energy_6h",
    predictionCol=f"{model_name.lower()}_prediction",
    metricName="rmse"  
)

RMSE is used as the evaluation metric since RegressionEvaluator does not support RMSLE [5].

In [123]:
# Parameter Grid for Hyperparameter Tuning
if model_name == "RandomForest":
    paramGrid = (
        ParamGridBuilder()
        .addGrid(base_model.maxDepth,              [5, 10, 15])
        .addGrid(base_model.featureSubsetStrategy, ['auto', 'sqrt', 'log2'])
        .build()
    )
else:  # Gradient Boosted Trees
    paramGrid = (
        ParamGridBuilder()
        .addGrid(base_model.maxDepth,        [3, 5, 7])     
        .addGrid(base_model.maxIter,         [50,100])
        .addGrid(base_model.stepSize,        [0.05, 0.1, 0.2])
        .build()
    )

For Random Forest, the model shows heavy reliance on a single feature (building_id_SI = 0.4453), as seen in the feature importance. To mitigate overfitting and improve generalization, minInstancesPerNode and featureSubsetStrategy are included in the parameter grid. These introduce regularization and feature randomness by limiting overly small splits and ensuring minimum features per node, thereby improving generalization.

In contrast, GBT distributes feature importance more evenly and does not require these adjustments. Similarly, numTrees is tuned only for Random Forest since it builds trees through bagging, whereas GBT grows trees sequentially.

Other Parameters:

  • maxIter: Number of boosting iterations (GBT) or trees (RF); higher values may capture more patterns but risk overfitting.

  • maxDepth: Controls tree complexity; deeper trees capture more detail but may overfit.

  • stepSize: Learning rate in boosting; regulates each tree’s contribution.

In [132]:
# Choose pipeline based on model name
pipeline = pipeline_gbt_selected if model_name == "GBT" else pipeline_rf_selected

# Create CrossValidator
crossval = CrossValidator(
    estimator=pipeline,
    estimatorParamMaps=paramGrid,
    evaluator=evaluator,
    numFolds=5,
    parallelism=4,
    seed=2025
)

Justification

  • Applied 5-fold cross-validation with PySpark’s CrossValidator to ensure robust generalization and reduce overfitting.

  • Evaluated the selected estimator (Random Forest or GBT pipeline) across a predefined hyperparameter grid.

  • Used RMSE as the evaluation metric, since RegressionEvaluator does not support RMSLE.

  • parallelism = 2 allows two parameter sets to be evaluated simultaneously, improving tuning efficiency. This aligns with the local[4] Spark configuration, utilizing available cores without overloading the system..

In [135]:
# Avoid recomputed every run by randomSplit()
feature_df.persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK) # [6]

# Selected Features
feature_selected = feature_df.select(important_features + ["energy_6h"])

# Create Train and Test Set [0.8,0.2]
train_selected, test_selected = feature_selected.randomSplit([0.8, 0.2], seed=2025)
In [125]:
# Fit cross-validated model
cvModel = crossval.fit(train_selected)
In [126]:
# Predictions on test
tuned_predictions = cvModel.transform(test_selected)
In [122]:
# Define the prediction column name dynamically
prediction_col = f"{model_name.lower()}_prediction"

# Compute tuned RMSLE
tuned_rmsle = rmsle(tuned_predictions, labelCol="energy_6h", predictionCol=prediction_col)

# Print result
print(f"Tuned {model_name} RMSLE: {tuned_rmsle:.4f}")

Tuned GBT RMSLE: 1.8874
In [141]:
# Access the best model
cv_best_model = cvModel.bestModel

# Get final stage from the best model (last stage in the pipeline)
cv_final_stage = cv_best_model.stages[-1]

# Extract parameter map
cv_param_map = cv_final_stage.extractParamMap()

# Convert to readable dictionary
cv_param_dict = {param.name: val for param, val in cv_param_map.items()}

# Print parameters nicely
for param, val in cv_param_dict.items():
    print(f"{param}: {val}")

cacheNodeIds: False
checkpointInterval: 10
featureSubsetStrategy: all
featuresCol: features
impurity: variance
labelCol: energy_6h
leafCol: 
lossType: squared
maxBins: 2048
maxDepth: 3
maxIter: 100
maxMemoryInMB: 256
minInfoGain: 0.0
minInstancesPerNode: 1
minWeightFractionPerNode: 0.0
predictionCol: gbt_prediction
seed: -1219566574311815711
stepSize: 0.05
subsamplingRate: 1.0
validationTol: 0.01
In [153]:
# Choose the better model based on RMSLE
if tuned_rmsle < selected_rmsle:
    best_model = cvModel.bestModel
    best_predictions = tuned_predictions
    best_rmsle = tuned_rmsle
    variant = "tuned"
    print(f"Selected best: Tuned {model_name} (RMSLE = {tuned_rmsle:.4f})")
else:
    best_model = selected_model
    best_predictions = selected_predictions
    best_rmsle = selected_rmsle
    variant = "selected"
    print(f"Selected best: Selected Features {model_name} (RMSLE = {selected_rmsle:.4f})")

Selected best: Selected Features GBT (RMSLE = 1.6161)
In [156]:
# Save the chosen best model
save_dir = f"models/{model_name.lower()}_{variant}_best"
best_model.write().overwrite().save(save_dir)

Model Tuning Discussion
The manually tuned GBT model using features with importance ≥ 0.3 outperformed the cross-validated model, achieving a lower RMSLE of 1.6161 compared to 1.8874. This suggests that simpler models with fewer, more informative features can generalize better than more complex configurations discovered via exhaustive grid search.

While the cross-validated model had a deeper hyperparameter search (e.g., maxIter=100, stepSize=0.05, maxDepth=3), it may have overfit or become too conservative. In contrast, the manually tuned model (maxDepth=5, stepSize=0.1, maxIter=20) struck a better bias-variance tradeoff, likely due to the cleaner feature space.

This outcome emphasizes that feature selection based on domain-driven importance thresholds can complement or even outperform automated tuning methods in practice.

Part 4. Data Ethics, Privacy, and Security ¶

In the era of big data, the convergence of vast quantities of information from various sources raises critical questions related to data ethics, privacy, and security. For example, in the case of privacy, many companies are collecting much more data than they need from customers. In our case, we used a real-world data set with real customer information. How do you utilise those datasets with ethics, privacy and security in mind?

In this part of the assignment, you are tasked to explore these topics within the context of big data processing, drawing on contemporary research, real-world examples, and ethical considerations.

(word limit: 500 words, please include references)

(mandatory): Define the concepts of data ethics, privacy, and security within the big data domain.

(Choose one or more topics, you can also create your own topic)
explain the significance of these issues in today’s data-driven world.

Data Ethics:

  • Analyse how data ethics can influence big data processing;
  • Examine real-world examples of how data ethics has been handled, both positively and negatively.
  • Analyse the balance between technological advancements and ethical responsibilities

Data Privacy:

  • Discuss the challenges and importance of maintaining privacy in big data.
  • Investigate regulations and laws that govern data privacy, such as GDPR.
  • Evaluate tools and techniques used to ensure privacy, and suggest improvements or new methodologies.

Data Security:

  • Explore the potential security risks associated with big data processing.
  • Assess the measures currently in place to secure big data, including encryption, authentication, and authorisation.

(mandatory) Summarise the key findings of your analysis.

Data Privacy in Big Data
In the big data domain, data privacy refers to individuals’ rights to control how their personal information is collected, used, and shared. Data ethics encompasses the principles and values guiding responsible data handling, including fairness, transparency, consent, and accountability [7]. Meanwhile, data security refers to the technical and organizational safeguards used to protect data against unauthorized access, corruption, or misuse: such as encryption, access controls, and audit logs [8].

These three concepts intersect critically in big data systems, especially when handling high-volume, high-variety, and high-velocity datasets. In this assignment, smart energy prediction models are trained on granular building-level features (e.g., building_id,energy_6h,primary_use,site_id) that may indirectly reveal sensitive behavioral or operational patterns. For example, consumption spikes may correlate with organizational activity, which poses re-identification risks if not properly anonymized.

Risks and Regulatory Landscape
The scale of big data can undermine traditional anonymization due to linkage attacks—where seemingly de-identified data can be cross-referenced with external datasets to re-identify individuals [9]. This is particularly relevant when analyzing latent features (latent_y,latent_s) that may encode proprietary or behavioral signals not explicitly known to users. Such data misuse contradicts the ethical principle of informed consent and violates regulatory standards like GDPR or CCPA [10].

Regulations such as the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR) mandate lawful data collection, purpose limitation, and “privacy by design” [11]. The GDPR also requires Data Protection Impact Assessments (DPIAs) when processing high-risk data, such as continuous smart meter readings. Meanwhile, ISO/IEC 27001 provides industry-wide standards for information security governance [12].

Privacy-Preserving Methods
Several methods can reduce privacy risks in big data modelling:

  • k-Anonymity, l-Diversity, and t-Closeness help ensure individual records cannot be uniquely identified by generalizing or grouping quasi-identifiers [13]-[15].

  • Differential privacy adds noise to outputs or queries to guarantee that no individual can be reverse-engineered from aggregate results [16].

  • Data masking, access control, and audit logging enhance data security in storage and transmission stages [17].

For this assignment, future improvements might involve applying differential privacy to features like latent_y before model training. In production systems, it would also be prudent to encrypt data at rest, monitor access during model runs, and avoid storing raw identifiers like building_id post-training.

Conclusion
Big data’s potential must be balanced with strong ethical, privacy, and security safeguards. In energy analytics and similar domains, practitioners must ensure models are not only performant but also aligned with ethical and legal obligations. Responsible data stewardship-supported by privacy-preserving technologies and regulatory compliance s essential for building trustworthy systems in the era of big data.
